observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management initiate the formal transfer of the
properties to the respective Barangays through the execution of a Deed of Donation
with prior approval from the Sangguniang Panlungsod and prospectively ensure
compliance with regulations on donated assets. Further, determine other properties in
the records of the City which are in the actual custody and used by other entities (aside
from barangays) and follow the same procedure in transferring these assets to the said
offices.

Henceforth, Management shall direct the City Accountant to prepare JEVs to
derecognize in the books of the City the assets that were transferred and donated; and
recognize in the books of the respective barangays the carrying amount of the assets
donated and transferred to them. Further, the CGSO shall also derecognize these
donated and transferred assets in their property records.

We recommended that Management:
a. Establish a policy in the capitalization of cost of repairs and other
subsequent costs of PPE which will be uniformly adopted by the CAO,
CGSO and Inventory Committee in compliance to the relative provisions of
MNGAS for LGUs Vol. III; and
b. Direct the CAO, in coordination with the CGSO, to further evaluate the
qualification of the existing capitalized cost of repairs in their records
(including the properties in the custody of and used by barangays as
discussed in AOM No. 2022-11(2021)) and thereafter, prepare adjusting
entries for the derecognition of those which do not meet the capitalization
requirements using the following pro-forma entry:
Account Debit Credit
Prior Years Adjustment xxx
Accumulated Depreciation – PPE Account xxx
PPE Account xxx

Further, we recommended that:
a. Management require the departments with unsubmitted initial inventory
count forms to submit immediately the same to the CGSO and to assist the
assigned Inventory Teams in the verification of properties in their respective
departments;
b. CGSO adopt the recommended forms of PCs (Annex C) for immovable
properties which may be modified or provided with additional information
at their discretion pending the approval of applicable PC forms; and
c. Management continuously follow the procedures stated in Sections 6.0 to 9.0
of COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the inventory taking, reconciliation of
inventory count per RPCPPE with GCSO and CAO, derecognition of non-
existing/missing PPEs without available record of accountability and
accounting entries and disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

Furthermore, we also reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the
City Accountant maintain PPELCs and RPLCs with the forms prescribed in Annexes
9 and 11 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II and designate a personnel who will
solely devote his/her time in preparing necessary property records and reconciliation
thereof with the RPCPPE and CGSO property records. Moreover, it is recommended
that the CGSO and City Engineering Office exhaust all efforts in retrieving the records
for immovable properties.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management require
the City Accountant to properly maintain the Subsidiary Ledgers for all fund transfers
to support their respective control accounts and utilize available records to obtain
details of the balances and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to ensure the
accuracy and validity of the balances presented in the financial statements.

Henceforth, we recommended that the inter-agency fund transfer amounting to
₱27,975,675.60 as of December 31, 2021 under the General Fund be transferred to the
Trust Fund and that the remaining amounts recorded erroneously as Due to NGAs be
adjusted to the proper accounts.

Further, we also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the
designated focal person of the Implementing Offices for every project/ program funded
by other government agencies and offices to coordinate with the City Accountant and
City Treasurer in order that receipts and utilization of Trust Funds can be accurately
recorded and monitored. We further recommended that the City Accountant and City
Treasurer always be furnished with the copy of the MOA for each and every
program/project in order to serve as the guidelines in the disbursement of the funds
and provide all the necessary information needed in the properly recording of the same.

Lastly, we recommended that the Management require the concerned
Implementing Offices to provide the Audit Team with copies of the MOA and other
pertinent documents relative to fund transfers by other government offices for our
verification.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that Management:
a. Instruct the IOs to properly coordinate with the Accounting and Treasury
Departments when a fund is about to be released by an NGA or other
sources so that proper accounting, recording and reporting thereof can be
made; and
b. Require the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries
after a thorough verification and analysis has been made thereof, and
collating the necessary supporting documents to substantiate the same, for
a fair presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a. City Treasurer to constantly send a written notice to the payees at least one
month before the checks becomes stale; and
b. The City Accountant review the long outstanding stale checks and take up
the necessary entry reversing to Cash-in Bank and Accounts Payable the
amounts thereof to ensure the fair presentation of the account balances in
the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require all Accountable Officers to fully
liquidate their Petty Cash Fund at the end of the year in accordance with Section 48 of
the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that Management require all Accountable Officers to settle
their respective cash advances at the end of the year to reflect the correct balances of
the accounts affected in the books of accounts of the Agency. Henceforth, avoid
granting subsequent cash advances to Accountable Officers with outstanding cash
advances and withhold the salaries and/or any amount due to those Accountable
Officers who were unable to comply with the pertinent rules and regulations on cash
advances. Adhere strictly to the guidelines in the granting, utilization and settlement
of cash advances as enunciated in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We further recommended the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash
advances amounting to ₱426,865.23 as of December 31, 2021 and submit the liquidation
report or proof of refund/s, if there is any, to the Auditor for post-audit. Send Demand
Letters to the concerned Accountable Officer(s), Agency Officials and employees with
unliquidated cash advances for monitoring purposes.

We recommended that Management request for write-off of receivable accounts
which were dormant for more than 10 years after all the remedies have been exhausted,
following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to:
a. Review and analyze the reconciling items of reciprocal accounts and take
up the necessary adjustments in the respective book of accounts;
b. Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts to ensure that all
reconciling items are resolve and/or adjusted; and
c. Perform constant review and appraisal of transactions to deter errors and
omissions.

We reiterated our last year’s recommendation that Management exert its best
effort to implement all programmed projects under the 20 percent EDF during the
current year to ensure that its objectives of desirable socio-economic and
environmental outcomes will be achieved.

Further, we recommended that Management expedite the implementation and
completion of the 32 unimplemented projects during the ensuing CY 2022.

We recommended that Management require the City Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Officer (CDRRMO) to prepare the LDRRMFIP with details as to the
programs/projects/activities to be funded by the 70 percent LDRRMF-CO, respectively
and follow the format in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 in the preparation of
the same and for approval by the LDRRMF Council/Sangguniang Panlungsod and to
be incorporated in the Local Development Plan and AIP.

Further, the City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC), likewise
present in the AIP, Local Development Plan (LDP), as well as in the Annual Work and
Financial Plan, the description, location and estimated budget of proposed
programs/projects/activities funded by the LDRRMF-MF(CO), similar to the
presentation of the projects funded by the 20 per cent Economic Development Fund
(EDF).

Finally, in the proposed Annual Budget, reflect the specific purpose of the
appropriations (details of the projects for implementation during the year), which will
be funded by the 70 per cent LDRRMF-MF(CO), as reflected in the LDRRMFIP, AIP
and LDP, instead of lump-sum appropriation, for the information of its users and to
enhance transparency in the operations of the City, pursuant to the afore-cited
pertinent Sections of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Section 306 of the Local
Government Code of 1991 or RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the City Government optimize the utilization of the
LDRRMF in order to strengthen its disaster preparedness and avoid potential damages
during disasters.

We recommended that Management must ensure the following:
a. That the surety bond issued by contractors as performance bonds on
infrastructure projects shall contain the specific dates of coverage,
specifically the date starting from the effectivity of the bond up to the time
of completion of the project and up to one year from completion, including
the expiration date thereof. Henceforth, surety bonds posted by contractors
with inconsistent or unclear stipulations relative to the validity period are
not accepted as Performance Bond; and
b. That projects with approved contract time extensions are supported with
evidence that the validity of the performance security is correspondingly
extended pursuant to Section 11.5 of Annex “E” of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management require the Inspectorate Team:
a. To immediately conduct a final inspection of completed projects which have
not yet passed the defects liability period for the purpose of final acceptance
and to determine the existence of defects and deficiencies for correction of
the contractor at their own expenses in accordance to Section 62.2.2.1 of the
Revised IRR of RA 9184. Thereafter, issue the corresponding Certificate of
Acceptance, after all defects/deficiencies, if any, were repaired by the
Contractor to the satisfaction of the Inspectorate Team and upon the
submission of the Warranty Certificate by the Contractor in accordance
with the provisions of 62.2.3.3 and 62.2.3.4 of the Revised IRR of Republic
Act No. 9184; and
b. To submit the issued Certificate of Acceptance after the conduct of the final
inspections and the corresponding warranty security posted by the
contractors to the Audit Team for verification.

Henceforth, to require the Inspectorate Team to conduct separate
inspections for the following:
a. Upon completion of the projects as basis for the issuance of the certificate
of completion.
b. During the defects liability period, if with reports or information of
defects/deficiencies noted in the projects, to require the contractor to
repair such at their own expenses in accordance to Section 62.2.2.1 of the
Revised IRR of RA 9184; and
c. At the end of the defects liability period, to determine if the project is
free from defects/deficiencies, or if there are any that were discovered
during the one year period, if these were already repaired/corrected by
the Contractor to the satisfaction of the Inspectorate Team.

Finally, for the Management to stop the practice of issuing only one certificate
for their projects for the completion, acceptance and turn-over. Instead, they shall issue
a Certificate of Project Completion and Turn-over upon completion of the project and
a Certificate of Acceptance only after all defects/deficiencies, if any, were repaired by
the Contractor to the satisfaction of the Inspectorate Team and upon the submission of
the Warranty Certificate by the contractor in accordance with the provisions of
62.2.3.3 and 62.2.3.4 of the Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to withhold
the uncollected and under-deducted retention money from the subsequent billings of
the contractors of on-going projects and henceforth, to strictly implement the proper
withholding of the retention money on partial billings by contractors, pursuant to the
above-mentioned regulations, unless otherwise, such as on the following cases:
a. If the 1st partial progress billing is more than 50 percent of the value of
works, the ten percent retention money shall be deducted only up to the 50
percent value of works of the project;
b. If the 1st partial progress billing is less than 50 percent of the value of works
and the ten percent retention money was deducted, the difference up to the
50 percent value of works from said 1st billing, shall still be deducted with
the ten percent retention money from the 2nd partial billing of more than 50
percent completion; and
c. For the 1st and final billing of 100 per cent completed project, the ten
percent retention money shall still be deducted only up to the 50 percent
value of works of the completed project.

Additionally, the City Accountant shall compute the retention based on the total
amount due to the contractor prior to any deduction in accordance with above-cited
regulations.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to release the
retention money for infrastructure projects in accordance to Section 6.2 of the “Annex
E” of the IRR of RA No, 9184.

Henceforth, compel all the contractors of the infrastructure projects of the City
Government whose defects liability period had not yet expired and whose retention
money were released prior to the lapse of the one year period after completion or was
not deducted to the first and final billing of 100% completed projects, as the case
maybe, without a valid substitute equivalent to the retention money as prescribed in
the IRR of RA No. 9184, to post the required financial instrument that is valid until the
end of the defects liability period, otherwise, demand that they refund or pay the
retention money for the projects.

Further, to instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to remind the bidders for
the contracts to be bidded by the City Government of the requirement of retention
money on projects as provided in the above cited provisions of the Revised IRR of RA
9184.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a. BAC to strictly enforce the BSD when bidders commit any act resulting in
the forfeiture of bid security under Sections 23.1 (b), 34.2, 40.1 and 69.1
except 69.1(f) of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, in accordance with GPPB
Resolution No. 15-2014, in order that bidders will strictly comply with the
requirements of the bidding and the awarding of contracts, hence,
implementation of the projects will not be delayed;
b. BAC to prepare and furnish a written demand to the erring bidders of the
subject project for the payment of the penalties as a result of the violation of
the conditions in the BSD. Henceforth, review all their previous bidding
activities and impose the penalties on erring bidders for any violation of the
BSD, if there are any; and
c. City Accountant to collect or to deduct from the amount due the bidders the
total penalties for the enforcement of the Bid Securing Declaration, if any.

We recommended that the Management continue to maximize the utilization of
GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-related PPAs in order to attain the
objective for which funds were provided. Likewise, adjust and propose new PPAs that
can adopt to the current situation brought about by the COVID 19-pandemic and asses
properly the expenses to implement the said PPAs in order to avoid provision of
excessive budget for each activity.

Henceforth, we recommended that City continue to implement programs and
projects in compliance to RA No. 9003 to protect the public health and the environment
of the City.

It is likewise recommended that the City should push through with the plan of
establishing its own Sanitary Land Fill (SLF).

We recommended and the Management agreed to submit the following:
a. A document of authority such as Office Orders or Memoranda,
indicating the names of persons who were assigned to COVID 19
treatment/quarantine facilities directly catering to or in contact with
COVID-19 patients, PUIs, and PUMs, and the period of their
assignment;
b. DTRs or duly accomplished time records of employees; and
c. Certified or authorized document by the head of agency/office
establishing that frontline HRHs physically reported for work at their
assigned work stations during the state of national emergency.

We recommended and the Management agreed to submit the following:
a. Documents of authority for the entitlement of the AHDP allowance such
as certification or office/assignment orders covering assignment of HRHs
to the City’s COVID 19 treatment/quarantine facilities, and whose official
duties and responsibilities are directly related to the healthcare response
of the government to COVID-19;
b. DTRs or duly accomplished time records of employees; and
c. Certified or authorized document by the head of agency/office
establishing that frontline HRHs physically reported for work at their
assigned work stations during the state of national emergency.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management instruct the City Accounting Office to take up the unliquidated fund transfers to the Barangays for the implementation of various barangay infrastructure projects as Due from LGUs under the asset account. 

Also, make a reversing entry of Accounts Payable (unreleased fund) and Subsidy to Other LGUs accounts for the unreleased fund and instead retain it as continuing appropriation, for proper monitoring and fair presentation in the financial statements. Henceforth, refrain from expensing outright the fund transfers for a specific purpose for which timely liquidation is required.

We recommended that Management require all transfer of funds to barangays for the implementation of projects and programs be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) and other documentary requirements as enumerated in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish proper accountability and monitoring of the proper utilization of funds. 

Further, we recommended that Management consider including a provision in the MOA that failure to liquidate the previous fund transfer in a given period shall forfeit the succeeding fund transfer intended for them and that the fund will be reverted to Unappropriated Surplus of the City after the lapse of deadline for the liquidation of the said fund transfer. 

Moreover, we recommended that the City Accountant review thoroughly the required documentation for fund transfer and require the recipient barangays to submit the liquidation and accomplishment reports to ensure that the funds are used for its intended purpose

We recommended that Management:

Compel the CSWD Officer to fast track the submission of the Relief Distribution Lists indicating the names of recipients and their signatures or, at the very least, signed by the Punong Barangay as recipient in behalf of the constituents, otherwise, non-submission thereof would warrant the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance; and 

Require the City Accountant to ensure that all claims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation to protect the interests of the government and to prevent unwarranted disbursements of public funds.

We recommended that Management adopt the recommended procedural flowchart in the procurement, recording and distribution of food supplies (Annex 22) pursuant to the afore-cited law and regulations.  The following are the recommended changes in the procedures:

The inspection of deliveries of food supplies purchased shall be conducted by the CGSO and shall be accepted in the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR). Deliveries of supplies shall be immediately recorded in the supplies stock card on the basis of the AIR and other supporting documents. The custody of the said food supplies shall be the responsibility of the CGSO until issuance thereof to the requesting party thru the CSWDO. 

The City Accountant shall adopt the perpetual inventory method in recording the food supplies procured. The AIR and other supporting documents shall be the basis in the preparation of the DV and recording of deliveries in the books and the appropriate ledger cards.   

A Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) (numbered based on RIS logbook) shall be prepared in three (3) copies to turn over the responsibility of the goods from the CGSO to the CSWDO during withdrawal of the supplies from the warehouse, distributed as follows: 

	 Copy 1- Accounting
       Copy 2- Requesting Party                   
       Copy 3- File Copy of CGSO          
 
The CSWDO shall be responsible for the proper distribution of the food supplies to the barangays. A sub-RIS in four (4) copies shall be prepared for the request for issuance of food supplies, distributed as follows:
                   
                   Copy 1- Accounting
                   Copy 2- Requesting Party                   
                   Copy 3- File Copy of CGSO
                   Copy 4- File copy of CSWDO
                
The CSWDO shall number the sub-RIS based on a sub-RIS Logbook maintained for the purpose.  The sub-RIS Logbook shall have the following column headings: sub-RIS No., Date, Description and Quantity of Food Supplies to be Issued, Name of Requesting Party, Position/Designation, Official Station or Address and Signature.

After properly accomplishing and signing the sub-RIS, the CSWDO shall issue the food supplies to the barangay officials authorized to the received the supplies.

The CSWDO shall submit to the CGSO, on a weekly basis or as necessary, the 1st and 3rd copy of the sub-RIS.  The CGSO shall promptly prepare the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) based on the RIS and sub-RIS which shall be submitted to the City Accountant for recording.

The City Accountant shall prepare a JEV to record the food supplies expense based on the SSMI received from the CGSO.

The CSWDO shall require all requesting parties in the sub-RIS to submit the RDL within 15 days after the distribution of the food supplies to the beneficiaries and shall conduct regular monitoring of the submission thereof and follow up with the concerned requesting party who failed to submit the RDL.

The CSWDO shall prepare and submit the liquidation report, once the RDLs are complete, to the City Accountant for submission to the Auditor and to monitor the liquidation thereof.  

The City Accountant and the CGSO shall regularly reconcile their respective records to determine any errors in recording of food supplies procurement and issuances.

The PPMPs shall be submitted by each Department/Office of the City Government to the City Budget Office and shall be evaluated and included in the proposed Annual Budget for approval. After approval of the proposed Annual Budget, the City Budget Office shall furnish a copy of the same, together with the corresponding PPMPs to the BAC Secretariat for its review and consolidation into the proposed APP. 

The BAC shall evaluate the APP and determine the applicable mode of procurement for each goods or services to be procured contained therein.  After the evaluation, the BAC shall issue a Resolution recommending the modes of procurement for all the items contained in the APP and forwards the same, with the APP to the Mayor for approval.  

A copy of the BAC Resolution, together with the approved APP shall be furnished the CGSO as basis for the preparation of Purchase Request on goods or services needed for the quarter at the beginning of the year and of each subsequent quarter.

Requisition procedures prescribed in Section 116 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Vol. I shall be strictly observed to avoid unnecessary procedures and documentary requirements which might hinder the timely procurement of needed supplies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the City Accountant to hasten the preparation and submission of
the BRS for CY 2020, together with the corresponding Journal Entry
Vouchers (JEVs), paid/cancelled checks and original copies of Bank
Statements and Debit and Credit Memos for review and verification; and
b. Make representation with the depository bank to ensure prompt delivery
of the Bank Statements to the LGU. Otherwise, secure copies of snapshots
of the bank statements to facilitate the preparation of the BRS.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that Management:
a. Require the City Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs)
to record in the books all the reconciling items, after a thorough
verification and analysis thereof, and collating the necessary supporting
documents to substantiate the same, for a fair presentation of the affected
accounts in the financial statements; and
b. Instruct the IOs to coordinate properly with the Accounting and Treasury
Departments when a fund is about to be released by an NGA or other
sources so that proper accounting, recording and reporting thereof can be
made.

We recommended that:
a. For the unclaimed checks that have become stale, the City Treasurer mark
cancelled on its face and forward the same to the City Accountant, together
with the DVs for recording. Henceforth, a written notice be sent at least a
month before the check becomes stale to payees of checks which have
remained unclaimed; and
b. The City Accountant, based on the BRS, revert the total amount of stale
checks outstanding to the Cash-in Bank and Accounts Payable accounts to
ensure the proper presentation of the account balances. Regarding the
unclaimed stale checks forwarded by the City Treasurer, the same shall be
set up as Accounts Payable.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the City Treasurer to identify the specific sources of funds and
establish whether these were essentially idle funds in accordance with
Section 21 and 22 of COA Circular No. 92-382; and
b. Hasten the implementation of the projects identified in the CDP-ELA
2020-2024 in order that the needed services to the constituents are not
delayed.

We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to obtain an
updated passbook from DBP – Legazpi for the Time Deposit accounts and
direct the City Accountant to reconcile the balance per books and actual bank balance and
prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) for the required adjustments.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management direct
the City Accountant to request for the write-off of the receivable accounts which have
remained dormant for 10 years or more and where settlement/collectability could no
longer be ascertained in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.
Further, the City Accountant to conduct regular monitoring and analysis of
receivable accounts to ensure that these are collected when these become due and
demandable and that cash advances and fund transfers are liquidated within the
prescribed period.

We recommended that the Management instruct the City Accounting Office to
take up the unliquidated fund transfers to the Barangays for the implementation of
various barangay infrastructure projects as Due from LGUs under the asset account.
Also, make a reversing entry of Accounts Payable (unreleased fund) and
Subsidy to Other LGUs accounts for the unreleased fund and instead retain it as
continuing appropriation, for proper monitoring and fair presentation in the
financial statements. Henceforth, refrain from expensing outright the fund transfers
for a specific purpose for which timely liquidation is required.

We recommended that Management require all transfer of funds to barangays
for the implementation of projects and programs be covered by a Memorandum of
Agreement (MOA) and other documentary requirements as enumerated in Section
3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish proper accountability and
monitoring of the proper utilization of funds.
Further, we recommended that Management consider including a provision in
the MOA that failure to liquidate the previous fund transfer in a given period shall
forfeit the succeeding fund transfer intended for them and that the fund will be
reverted to Unappropriated Surplus of the City after the lapse of deadline for the
liquidation of the said fund transfer.
Moreover, we recommended that the City Accountant review thoroughly the
required documentation for fund transfer and require the recipient barangays to
submit the liquidation and accomplishment reports to ensure that the funds are used
for its intended purpose.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
a. Exhaust all efforts to retrieve the record, reports and source documents
that would establish details of the recorded lump-sum prior years’ balance
and the entries/postings without explanation in order that the balances
appearing in the SLs may be ascertained; and
b. Conduct monthly reconciliation of reciprocal accounts to ascertain
whether payments, transfers, liquidations/settlement and adjustments were
properly recorded by the accounting personnel concerned and to avoid any
discrepancy in their records.

We recommended that Management form a Reconciliation Team composed of
least one capable member coming from the CAO, CGSO and City Engineering Office
(for the infrastructure assets, buildings and other structures) who shall be
temporarily relieved of their regular duties until such time that the reconciliation
process is completed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Engineer to
provide the CGSO with the relevant information, documents and records such as
Certificate of Completion and Acceptance and As-Built Plans of all existing
constructed and acquired properties of the City as bases for recording.
Further, immediately or on regular basis, inform the CGSO of all the
completed projects after their completion and final acceptance and provide to him
copies of relevant documents and records thereof.
Also, the CGSO and the City Assessor shall maintain duplicate copies of the
OCTs/TCTs and other relevant documents of the real properties owned by the City
Government.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to determine and indicate in the RPCPPE the
physical condition of the infrastructure assets, buildings and other
structures for proper measurement of its carrying amount in the
financial statements and to update the current use of lots/land for
proper classification in the books by the City Accountant;
b. Further, the Inventory Committee to classify all the projects in the
RPCPPE consistent with the account classification in the books of
accounts and financial statements to facilitate the reconciliation of the
two records; and
c. The CGSO to assign the corresponding Property Name and Number
for items of infrastructure assets and buildings and other structures for
proper identification.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to attach the Inventory Tag/Stickers to the movable
properties immediately after the physical inventory;
b. CGSO to renew and update all the AREs and check whether all movable
properties with updated AREs were included in the RPCPPE and to
require the accountable officer/s to present the PPE item/s, if any, were not
included in the physical count by the Inventory Committee; and
c. Inventory Committee to indicate and properly identify the accountable
officer for each item of property in the RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Accountant
and the City General Services Officer to exhaust all efforts to retrieve the
records/reports and source documents to establish details of the recorded lump-sum
prior years’ balance for CYs 2007 and prior years, including the entries/postings
without explanations, in order that the balances appearing in the SLs may be
ascertained.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the City Accountant and CGSO to retrieve the records/reports
and source documents that would establish details of the irreconcilable
items in the SL and RPCPPE and determine the adjustments that
would be recorded in the books;
b. Instruct the City Accountant to record in the books the land which was
included in the RPCPPE amounting to ₱2.18 million. Likewise,
instruct the Inventory Team and the CGSO to update the RPCPPE
and records, respectively, for the Land recorded in the books
amounting to ₱8.58 million;
c. Determine the fair market values of the 10 lots with a total area of
42,628.75 square meters which were included in the submitted
RPCPPE but not recorded in the books and the five lots with a total
area of 90,644.00 square meters included in the CTO inventory of land
owned by the LGU and with title in the of the City as basis for
recording in the books of accounts;
d. Instruct the City Assessor to investigate and determine the proof of
ownership of the LGU on the 120 lots with total area 799,237.00 square
meters with tax declaration in their records in the name of the City.
Thereafter, determine its fair market value to be used in recording in
the books by the City Accountant and updating of the RPCPPE by the
Inventory Committee and records of the CGSO.
e. Conduct a comprehensive inventory of all its real properties and
ascertain which are covered with OCTs/TCTs;
f. For titled properties but the original copies of the OCTs/TCTs thereof
could not be located, initiate the process to reconstitute the titles
thereto;
g. For untitled properties, conduct cadastral surveys thereof and expedite
the processing of the titles thereto in the name of the City; and
h. Instruct the City Accounting Office and the CGSO to maintain
complete subsidiary records based on the inventory conducted and
perform regular reconciliation of the balances thereof.

We recommended that Management instruct the City General Service Officer
and City Accountant to maintain complete PC for each category of PPE and RPLC
for all real/immovable properties owned by the City following the prescribed forms in
the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that the Management instruct the:
a. City Engineer to provide the City Accountant with copy of the Certificate
of Project Completion and Acceptance of the completed projects
amounting to ₱29.2 million, to serve as basis by the City Accountant in
recording completed projects as PPE;
b. City Engineer and City Accountant make a reconciliation of the completed
and on-going projects annually as of year-end to ensure that completed
projects are properly closed to the appropriate PPE account. Any unpaid
amount due to delay of the submission of billing by the contractor should
be recognized as payable;
c. City Accountant to provide the periodic depreciation charges for these
completed projects to allow fair presentation of the affected accounts in the
year-end financial statements;
d. City Engineer assign Project Number for all projects to be implemented by
the City for easy identification, monitoring and recording the same;
e. City Accountant make the necessary correcting entries to revert the cost of
Construction of City Hall- Phase I and accumulated depreciation
recorded; and
f. City Accountant to transfer the CY 2020 year-end CIP balances, from the
one SL card maintained for CIPs by the CAO to the required CIPLC,
reconstructing the CIP balances per specific type of the asset constructed
per Chart of Accounts. Henceforth, to maintain the appropriate CIPLC,
for each specific type of the asset constructed, whether thru straight
contract or by administration. Also, to ensure that all the required data in
the CIPLC are properly indicated therein and upon completion of the
project, reclassify the total CIP cost to the proper asset account using the
data in the CIPLC as reference.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the CGSO to
facilitate the immediate disposal of the aforementioned unserviceable properties and
waste materials to recoup any salvage value thereof. Specifically, we reiterate the
following measures:
a. Create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee, whose members are
required to attend relevant trainings on property management,
particularly on the disposal of unserviceable properties and waste
materials, should they lack the necessary technical competence;
b. Conduct an appraisal of the unserviceable properties and waste materials
and furnish the auditor a copy of the appraisal report thereof; and
c. Dispose the unserviceable properties based on the most appropriate mode
of disposal pursuant to the provisions of Sections 379 to 381 of RA No.
7160 and COA Circular Nos. 89-296 and 92-386 following the procedures
shown in flow chart of the disposal of property (Annex 16).
Further, we recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to
derecognize the costs of these properties once the disposal thereof has been
completed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:
a. The Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of the local roads under
the LGU’s jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of
Road Network System;
b. The General Service Officer to render a report on Local Road Network at
the end of the accounting period, maintain a Local Road Inventory and
Road Map and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Cards for
all roads and its components;
c. The City Engineer to provide the City Accountant the complete description
and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
d. The City Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads
and road components for every road network and reconcile the same to the
result of the physical inventory taking at the end of each year; and
e. The City Accountant to include the necessary disclosure on the total road
network system in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to make the necessary
reversing entry recording the Accounts Payable. Henceforth, strictly observe the
proper recognition of Accounts Payable by crediting this account only when the
goods are delivered or services are rendered pursuant to NGAS for LGUs, Vol. III.

We recommended that Management continue to pursue improved practices in
programming, prioritizing and implementing programs and projects to maximize the
utilization of the Local Development Fund in attaining desirable socio-economic
development and environmental management outcomes for the benefit of the
community.
Further, we recommended that Management expedite the implementation and
completion of the 32 unimplemented and nine on-going projects, respectively, during
the ensuing CY 2021.

We recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the City Accountant to present in the Report on the Sources
and Utilization of DRRMF important information such as the balances
of DRRMF per year (current year and transfers to the STF of each
previous year) and Continuing Appropriations for DRRM-CO for
effective monitoring of the balances and utilization of the Fund (please
refer to the attached proposed format per Annex 20).
b. Instruct the City Accountant and the Budget Officer to provide the
necessary details regarding the utilization of the fund such as
OBR/DV/JEV/Check Number in the report itself or in a separate
supporting schedule to facilitate its verification against the books of the
LGU.
We also reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that
Management instruct the City Accountant and City Budget Officer to reconcile their
records on the utilization of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF from the previous
years up to CY 2019 in order that separate subsidiary records may be established and
maintained for the unexpended balance of the DRRMF to the Special Trust Fund on
a yearly basis to serve as reference in the preparation of the Report on the Sources
and Utilization of DRRMF.

We commended the City Government for complying with the posting and
reporting requirements as required in Items 4.2, 4.3 and 4.4 of DBM LBC No. 125.
Further, we recommended that, henceforth, Management strictly comply with the
posting requirement of the procurements undertaken through Emergency
Procurement under the Bayanihan Act on the GPPB Online Portal to enhance
transparency and accountability.

We recommended that Management:
a. Compel the CSWD Officer to fast track the submission of the Relief
Distribution Lists indicating the names of recipients and their signatures
or, at the very least, signed by the Punong Barangay as recipient in behalf
of the constituents, otherwise, non-submission thereof would warrant the
issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance; and
b. Require the City Accountant to ensure that all claims against government
funds shall be supported with complete documentation to protect the
interests of the government and to prevent unwarranted disbursements of
public funds.

We recommended that Management:
a. Adopt prescribed control procedures in the receipt, inventory and issuance
of donations- in kind, and record the proper cost of the ending inventory
based on the value declared by the donor, if available, or using the
prevailing market price of the donated items;
b. Require the concerned offices/units to properly categorize the Donations- in
Kind received by the City and intended for COVID-19 response reported in
the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances for
consolidation;
c. Require the CDRRMO to submit a consolidated report on Summary/List of
Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, separately for Cash and In-
Kind transactions, to the NDRRMC through the OCD, copy furnished the
Auditor, as required in COA Circular No. 2014-009;
d. Require the Property/Supply Officer to maintain Registry of Donated Relief
Goods for DRRM to properly account for the ending inventory balances; and
e. Require the City Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial
Statements information pertaining to DRRM not evident on the face of the
financial statements.

We recommended that Management adopt the recommended procedural
flowchart in the procurement, recording and distribution of food supplies (Annex 22)
pursuant to the afore-cited law and regulations. The following are the recommended
changes in the procedures:
a. The inspection of deliveries of food supplies purchased shall be conducted
by the CGSO and shall be accepted in the Acceptance and Inspection
Report (AIR). Deliveries of supplies shall be immediately recorded in the
supplies stock card on the basis of the AIR and other supporting
documents. The custody of the said food supplies shall be the
responsibility of the CGSO until issuance thereof to the requesting party
thru the CSWDO.
b. The City Accountant shall adopt the perpetual inventory method in
recording the food supplies procured. The AIR and other supporting
documents shall be the basis in the preparation of the DV and recording
of deliveries in the books and the appropriate ledger cards.
c. A Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) (numbered based on RIS logbook) shall be
prepared in three (3) copies to turn over the responsibility of the goods
from the CGSO to the CSWDO during withdrawal of the supplies from
the warehouse, distributed as follows:
Copy 1- Accounting
Copy 2- Requesting Party
Copy 3- File Copy of CGSO
d. The CSWDO shall be responsible for the proper distribution of the food
supplies to the barangays. A sub-RIS in four (4) copies shall be prepared
for the request for issuance of food supplies, distributed as follows:
Copy 1- Accounting
Copy 2- Requesting Party
Copy 3- File Copy of CGSO
Copy 4- File copy of CSWDO
e. The CSWDO shall number the sub-RIS based on a sub-RIS Logbook
maintained for the purpose. The sub-RIS Logbook shall have the
following column headings: sub-RIS No., Date, Description and
Quantity of Food Supplies to be Issued, Name of Requesting Party,
Position/Designation, Official Station or Address and Signature.
f. After properly accomplishing and signing the sub-RIS, the CSWDO
shall issue the food supplies to the barangay officials authorized to the
received the supplies.
g. The CSWDO shall submit to the CGSO, on a weekly basis or as
necessary, the 1st and 3rd copy of the sub-RIS. The CGSO shall
promptly prepare the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued
(SSMI) based on the RIS and sub-RIS which shall be submitted to the
City Accountant for recording.
h. The City Accountant shall prepare a JEV to record the food supplies
expense based on the SSMI received from the CGSO.i. The CSWDO shall require all requesting parties in the sub-RIS to
submit the RDL within 15 days after the distribution of the food supplies
to the beneficiaries and shall conduct regular monitoring of the
submission thereof and follow up with the concerned requesting party
who failed to submit the RDL.
j. The CSWDO shall prepare and submit the liquidation report, once the
RDLs are complete, to the City Accountant for submission to the
Auditor and to monitor the liquidation thereof.
k. The City Accountant and the CGSO shall regularly reconcile their
respective records to determine any errors in recording of food supplies
procurement and issuances.
l. The PPMPs shall be submitted by each Department/Office of the City
Government to the City Budget Office and shall be evaluated and
included in the proposed Annual Budget for approval. After approval of
the proposed Annual Budget, the City Budget Office shall furnish a copy
of the same, together with the corresponding PPMPs to the BAC
Secretariat for its review and consolidation into the proposed APP.
m. The BAC shall evaluate the APP and determine the applicable mode of
procurement for each goods or services to be procured contained
therein. After the evaluation, the BAC shall issue a Resolution
recommending the modes of procurement for all the items contained in
the APP and forwards the same, with the APP to the Mayor for
approval.
n. A copy of the BAC Resolution, together with the approved APP shall be
furnished the CGSO as basis for the preparation of Purchase Request on
goods or services needed for the quarter at the beginning of the year and
of each subsequent quarter.
o. Requisition procedures prescribed in Section 116 of NGAS Manual for
LGUs, Vol. I shall be strictly observed to avoid unnecessary procedures
and documentary requirements which might hinder the timely
procurement of needed supplies.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the City Accountant to prepare and submit the Monthly Trial

and the 4th Quarter of CY 2020 for General Fund, respectively, and the

the abovementioned existing rules and regulations; and
b. Take appropriate immediate remedial measures, such as allowing the
Accounting personnel to render overtime, subject to the availability of
funds, in order to comply with the timely preparation and submission of
these mandatory financial reports, pursuant to mandated deadlines.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that Management:
a. Require the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and
financial reports to the City Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the
abovementioned existing law, rules and regulations;
b. Direct the City Treasurer to submit to the City Accounting Office all the
disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the
corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the
ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of
rules and regulations, as applicable; and
c. Direct the City Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the
recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the
ensuing month.

and maintain the Index of Payment for its employees, suppliers, and other agency

least five per cent of the total annual budget for the implementation of GAD-related
programs, projects and activities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GAD Focal Person
to:

b. Prepare and submit the GAD Accomplishment Report, including the
related supporting reports enumerated in Section 10.4 of PCW-NEDA-
DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-001, consistent with the DILG endorsed
GPB and enacted LGU budget; and
c. Henceforth, the LGU, thru their Local Chief Executive, inform the
concerned DILG Office in writing if there are changes in the DILG-
endorsed GPB and/or if there is a need to implement additional PPAs
relevant to current gender issues or GAD-related undertakings.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant
to:

schedules.
Also, we recommended that the City Accountant conduct the necessary
reconciliation of the Due to PhilHealth account to identify the composition of its
negative balance and accordingly make the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended that Management instruct the General Services Officer:

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that:
The City Accountant make an analysis of existing balances in the Due to NGAs accounts to determine the accuracy and validity of the account balances and prepare adjusting entries as necessary;
The designated Focal Person for every project/program funded by other government departments and offices coordinate with the City Accounting Office in order that receipts and utilization of trust funds can be accurately recorded and monitored. We further recommend that the concerned offices shall always furnish the CAO with a copy the MOA for each and every project/program in order to serve as reference in the disbursement of funds and provide all the necessary information needed in the recording of the same; and
The CAO properly maintain the necessary SLs to facilitate proper recording of financial transactions and to aid in the monitoring of the utilization of funds and status of projects and programs funded from National Government Agencies.

We recommended that Management:
Follow the guidelines and procedures prescribed in Section 8 of COA Circular 2016-005 on the write-off of the ₱374,010 dormant balance in the Due from LGUs account for the fair presentation of the account in the financial statements; and
Request the concerned barangays to submit to the CAO the necessary reports and documents to facilitate the recording of liquidation of outstanding receivables totaling ₱170,280.

We recommended that:
The GSO keep a complete and updated LRNPCs to serve as a means of checking the accuracy of the Road Network account maintained by the City Accountant;
Both the GSO and the City Accountant conduct regular reconciliation of the balances of their records, at least on a quarterly basis, to ensure that their records are consistent with each other; and
The City Engineer be required to furnish the City Accountant and the GSO complete description and cost segregation of road components for completed road projects as basis for recording in their respective subsidiary records.

We recommended that Management require the:
Inventory Committee/Team to conduct a thorough and complete physical inventory of the City’s properties, particularly Land Improvements, Infrastructure Assets and Building & Other Structures.  Thereafter, prepare the RPCPPE in the prescribed form (Annex 49, NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II) and submit the same to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year;
City Accountant and GSO to maintain PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, in accordance with the forms prescribed in Annexes 9 and 37 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II, respectively and conduct periodic reconciliation of the accounting and property records; and
City Accountant to prepare the Depreciation Schedule in a manner that the PPEs are described therein clearly and concisely using the Chart of Accounts as reference to facilitate reconciliation and cross-checking thereof with the RPCPPE.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with existing guidelines on LDRRMF as to the allowable expenditures chargeable against the fund to avoid disallowances.  Further, provide adequate budget for the purchase of Office Equipment and Furniture and Fixtures and other capital expenditures for use by the CDRRMO.
We recommended that the City Accountant transfer the unutilized balances for CY 2019 and the previous years of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the STF account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-12.

We recommended that the:
LDRMM Officer submit a copy of the LDRRM Council recommendation of the Revised LDRRMFIP and the LDRRMFIP-Continuing (CY 2014-2018) and the corresponding SP resolution for the approval of the plans to the Auditor for verification;
LDRMM Officer immediately prepare a revised LDRRMFIP for CY 2020 incorporating the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱40.21 million (See Annex 15 for computation) after its transfer to the STF to maximize the utilization of the fund; and
City Budget Officer maintain a RAAO for LDRRMF for CO and thereafter prepare the SAAOB, copy furnished the Auditor.

We recommended that Management allocate at least 1 percent of its DRRM funds for MDM and integrate in their LDRRM Plan policies, standards, guidelines, systems and procedures for MDM operations and research and development.

We recommended that Management require the Human Resource Management Officer:
Use the running balance of the leave credits to determine the actual number of vacation leave credits that the employee may use for monetization; and
Exhaust the VL credits first before SL credits may be used for monetization.

In addition, we recommended that Management review their income targets for Special Levy on Idle Lands, Franchise Tax and Other Taxes account to arrive at a more realistic income targets in the succeeding year.

We recommended that the City allocate at least 5 percent of the total annual budget for the implementation of GAD-related activities as required under Section 36 (a) of RA No. 9710 or the Magna Carta of Women.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GAD Focal Person to prepare and submit the GAD AR with the related supporting reports enumerated, within the prescribed period to facilitate the timely evaluation of the City’s performance in addressing gender issues and concerns.
We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to make a thorough analysis of the unremitted withholding taxes and remit to the BIR without further delay any amount due.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to make a thorough analysis of the unremitted contributions and loans repayment of officials and employees to GSIS, Philhealth and Pag-IBIG and to remit the same without further delay.
Further, in case penalties are imposed by the concerned agencies on the delayed remittance of the mandatory deductions, it shall be for the personal account of the City Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management continue their efforts and activities that contribute to the attainment of the income targets while undertaking the necessary measures to increase the collections of income accounts that did not meet its target.

We commended Management for these achievements and recommended to continue their commitment in improving their practices of proper SWM for the preservation of the environment and most importantly, ensuring the protection of public health.

We recommended that Management take the following immediate courses of action:
Require the City Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs)  to record in the books all the reconciling items, after a thorough analysis thereof, and collating the necessary supporting documents to substantiate the same, for a fair presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements;
Direct the City Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to submit and record in the books the eight unrecorded DVs totaling ₱578,351 and thereafter to submit the same to the Office of the Auditor for audit purposes, otherwise, Notice of Suspension or Disallowance shall be issued on said accounts; and
Require the Implementing Offices to coordinate properly with the Accounting and Treasury Department when a fund is about to be released by a National Government Agency so that proper accounting, recording and reporting thereof can be made.

We recommended that Management:
Investigate and reconcile the difference between the RPCPPE and accounting records amounting to ₱117.19 million and thereafter instruct the Inventory Team to conduct a thorough and complete physical inventory of all the City’s properties; and
Require the General Services Officer to maintain Property Cards and City Accountant to update and reconcile the PPELC, in accordance with the forms prescribed in Annexes 9 and 37 of the NGAs Manual for LGUs, Volume II, respectively and conduct periodic reconciliation of accounting and property records.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to compute for the depreciation of these assets and to record the same in the books of accounts, to allow fair presentation of the PPE account in the year-end financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
Require the submission of sufficient and appropriate documentation when granting financial assistance; and
Consider procuring the materials for the events/activities organized by the City subject to specific guidelines and their budgetary capability instead of granting financial assistance to the participants.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant makes an analysis of existing balances in the Due to NGAs accounts to determine the accuracy and validity of the account balances and prepare adjusting entries as necessary;
The designated Focal Person for every project/program funded by other government departments and offices coordinate with the City Accounting Office in order that receipts and utilization of Trust Funds can be accurately recorded and monitored. We further recommended that the City Accounting Office be always furnished with the MOA for each and every project/program in order to serve as the guidelines in the dispensing of funds and provide all the necessary information needed in the recording of the same; and
The City Accounting Office properly maintain the necessary SLs to facilitate proper recording of financial transactions and to aid in the monitoring of the utilization of funds and status of projects and programs funded from National Government Agencies.

We recommended that Management:
Implement BUB projects and programs in the most timely manner possible in order that the benefits to be derived therefrom may be enjoyed by the intended beneficiaries at the earliest possible time; and
Secure certification of completion and/or acceptance for the fully implemented projects in order that the completion of the project may already be reflected in accounting records.

We recommended that Management direct its officers to:
Fully implement GAD activities and programs;
Avoid over estimations in the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget; and
Make the necessary adjustments to the GAD Plan and Budget as the need arises, in order to fully maximize the potential benefits that the intended beneficiaries can derive therefrom.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod revisit/revise the 2007 RTC of the City of Ligao, particularly Section 108, Article I, Chapter 5 of the Code, which provides uniform rates for the assessment levels to be applied to the fair market value of real property, through an Ordinance and follow the above-cited rates stated in Section 218 of RA No. 7160 in determining the assessed value of the Buildings and Other Structures and Machinery for taxation purposes, to assess and collect the correct taxes due from taxpayers.
Moreover, we recommended that assessment and collection of taxes must be based on what was provided for in the existing RTC of the City to avoid illegal exaction.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod and concerned officials revisit the provisions of the Revenue Tax Code (RTC) of Ligao City to make possible amendments that would redound to the best interest of the City and the citizenry without going beyond the provisions and requirements of the LGC and its IRR.

We recommended that Sangguniang Panlungsod and the officials concerned take the necessary actions for enactment of the ordinance adopting the Schedule of Fair Market Values of real properties in the City and publication of the same to avoid further delay in the process of revision and its effectivity.

We recommended that the City Assessor request the Registrar of Deeds of Ligao City to furnish their Office a copy of the abstract of the Registry of Property, accompanied by copies of corresponding deeds of sale, donation, or partition, or other forms of alienation pursuant to the above-cited Manual, for CY 2018 and years thereafter annually or secure from them the said Registry every year.

We recommended that Management require/direct:
The City Treasurer to submit to the City Accountant the duly certified list of taxpayers showing the name of the taxpayers and amount due and collectible for CY 2019 and at the beginning of each year; and
The City Accountant to closely coordinate with the City Treasurer for the proper set-up of the RPT and SET Receivables, in consonance with Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
We recommended that Management give priority for the revision and update of the 2007 City RTC pursuant to Section 191 of the LGC of 1991.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Building Official or the City Engineer to review and conduct inspection of firms/establishments that obtained a Building Permit during the year to determine whether the building is not being occupied or is not being utilized pending issuance of a corresponding Certificate of Occupancy and inform the owners of the firms/establishments in writing, about the result of their review and inspection and require them to apply for Certificate of Occupancy, if warranted; and
Require the City Engineer to prepare and submit monthly to the City Assessor the List of Building Permittees and Certificate of Occupancy issued by their Office in order for the City Assessor to be able also to conduct inspection for the issuance of Tax Declaration on the Building/Structure issued with Certificate of Occupancy.

We recommended that Management:

Require the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Treasurer and thereafter to submit to the Accounting Office all the disbursement vouchers and payrolls, officials receipts, including the corresponding reports of each month on or before the fifth day  of the ensuing month or pursuant to the aforementioned pertinent provisions of laws, rules and regulations, as applicable;

Direct the Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor, within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month, as required by the aforementioned existing law, rules and regulations and into the possibility of allowing them to render overtime, subject to the availability of funds, in order to submit the accounts and financial reports on the mandated deadlines; and

Initiate the necessary administrative and / or criminal action in case of unjustified failure / refusal to effect compliance with the foregoing requirements by subordinate officials.

We recommended that the City Accountant cause the immediate
submission of all unsubmitted CY 2016 Reports including the Disbursement and
Collection Vouchers and supporting documents to the Auditor, for verification
and post-audit. Non-submission of these reports/accounts/documents shall
warrant the issuance of Notice of Suspension. Thereafter, strictly observe and
comply with the prescribed timeline on the submission of reports and accounts to

the Auditor as provided in Section 7.2.1a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated
September 15, 2009,

We recommended that Management:

a) direct the City Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the
year-end Financial Statements and supporting schedules for CY 2016
to expedite the timely review and verification thereof by the Andit
Team. Henceforth, strictly comply with the timeline on the
submission of the Agency’s Trial Balances and Financial Statements
and its supporting documents as required in COA Circular No. 2010-
001; and;

b) cause the suspension of payment of the salaries of the City Accountant
and other responsible officials for their failure to submit the Year-End
Financial Statements and supporting schedules for CY 2016 of the
Agency, until they have complied with the requirements of the
Commission on Audit pursuant to the aforecited law, rules and
regulations.

We recommended that the City Accountant submit the required
Supporting Schedules for the subject accounts in the year-end Financial
Statements for andit and verification.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to
maintain a complete set of subsidiary ledgers and prepare financial statements
for the above mentioned Special Accounts in the General Fund pursuant to
Section 313 of RA 7160 and pertinent provisions of NGAS Manual, Vol. L for
monitoring and control purposes.

We recommended that the City Accountant record immediately these
transactions in the books and adjust the affected accounts to allow the fair
presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements. Likewise,
submit the Disbursement Vouchers of these transactions to the Auditor for audit
purposes, otherwise this warrant the issuance of Notice of Suspension or
Disallowances. Henceforth, follow strictly the provisions of Section 7.2.1.a) of
COA Circular No. 2009-006 by recording immediately in the books of accounts
all transactions of the City.

We recommended that Management:

a) Request the concerned Officers and Employees to liquidate/settle
immediately their outstanding Cash Advances by demanding the
immediate submission of duly accomplished liquidation reports,
otherwise, withhold any money due them;

b) Strengthen the operation of preventive and monitoring controls to
ensure timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting
of balances of Cash Advances by monitoring strictly the period of
liquidating Cash Advances; and

¢) Comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated
February 10, 1997 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of Cash
Advances.

We recommended the following courses of actions, to address the
deficiencies noted:

a) Management takes appropriate action to resolve this recurring
deficiency considering the significance of the affected account in the
financial statements of the City;

b) Management consider assigning additional personnel in the accounting
who will maintain the subsidiary ledger pertaining to the Loans
Receivable — Others account;

¢) Accounting department maintains subsidiary ledgers and aging that
will aid management in monitoring and collecting its loans receivable.
Regularly reconcile the balances with the GL balances and update
recording/posting of transactions at all times;

d) Accounting department conduct reconciliation with the CCDO and
CAO receivable records, if any, and adjust the books, if necessary.

¢) Management to conduct a thorough evaluation of the existing loans
receivable, intensify collection efforts and institute legal remedies, if
warranted. Furthermore, strengthen internal control system over the
grant, monitoring and collection of loan assistance to ensure the
sustainability of the program.

We recommended that management direct the City Accountant to reconcile
the reciprocal accounts and effect adjustments to reflect the correct account
balances and present reliable data on the City’s financial operation as of a
given period of time.

We recommended the following courses of actions to correct the noted
deficiencies:

a). The General Services Officer to initiate the conduct of physical
inventory taking of all Supplies of the City Government at year-end
and prepare and submit the corresponding Reports of Physical Count
of Inventory (RPCI) to the Audit Team to establish the existence,
accuracy and validity of reported balances in the financial statements;
and

b). The City Accountant and General Service Officer maintain a complete
and updated Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards and regularly
conduct reconciliation thereof so that any discrepancies noted could be
promptly adjusted and reconciled.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the person(s) concerned to
prepare and submit the Physical and Financial Plan for the Confidential Fund
following the prescribed format of the plan in Annex A of Section 4.2 of COA-
DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01.

We recommended that Management rcquire the Contractors to post
additional performance security to cover the entire Defects Liability Period of the
abovementioned projects, otherwise, this warrant the issuance of Notice of
Suspension or Disallowance. To ensure posting thereof, Management shall not
issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance, pending compliance by the Contractors.

Henceforth, in future infrastructure projects, we recommend that the Bids
and Awards Committee (BAC) require the Contractors to post performance
securities to cover the period until the issuance of the Certificate of Final
Acceptance as a condition prior to the signing of the contracts.

We recommended that the BAC prepare the PMR with approval of the
HOPE and submit the same to the GPPB in the prescribed format within 14
calendar days after the end of every semester, as required under Section 12.2 of
the RTRR of RA No. 9184,

Further, we suggested that the Office of the Auditor be furnished with a
printed copy of the said monitoring report as submitted to the GPPB.

We recommended Management, especially the members of the BAC, BAC
Secretariat and its Technical Working Group (TWG) to:

a) Conduct a detailed review of its actual Procurement practice and exert
effort to align the same with the ideal Procurement timelines set forth
under Annex “C” of the amended IRR of RA No. 9184; and

b) Adopt an operational manual applicable within the authorized
functions of the BAC,

We recommended that copies of perfected contracts for goods and
infrastructure projects together with their supporting documents be provided to
the Auditor within five working days from execution thereof to facilitate timely
review and evaluation thereof.

a) Plan thoroughly the budget for the 20 per cent DF to maximize the
implementation of development projects to ensure that optimum
benefits are derived by the greater number of its constituents; and

b) Evaluate / assess the identified development projects under the
